content.text:
{
  "module": "database-api",
  "scores": {
    "visual_polish": 15,
    "domain_depth": 62,
    "data_richness": 55,
    "interaction_quality": 45,
    "ai_integration": 30,
    "mobile_first": 10,
    "performance": 58,
    "typescript_quality": 52,
    "error_handling": 68,
    "realtime": 40,
    "accessibility": 15,
    "security": 60,
    "differentiation": 35,
    "enterprise_readiness": 50
  },
  "overall_score": 42,
  "issues": [
    {
      "id": "database-api-C1-001",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "typescript_quality",
      "title": "Circular/broken imports in api/client.ts — supabase imported from itself and transformSupabaseError imported from wrong path in metrics.ts",
      "file": "src/api/endpoints/metrics.ts",
      "description": "metrics.ts imports `supabase` and `transformSupabaseError` from `../client`, but client.ts exports `supabaseQuery`/`supabaseMutation`/`createScopedClient` — not the raw `supabase` instance or `transformSupabaseError`. The raw client lives in `src/lib/supabase.ts` and `transformSupabaseError` lives in `src/api/errors.ts`. This causes TypeScript import resolution failures or runtime undefined references.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/api/endpoints/metrics.ts, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The file imports `supabase` and `transformSupabaseError` from `../client` on line 1 (`import { supabase, transformSupabaseError } from '../client'`). The client.ts file does NOT export a named `supabase` binding or `transformSupabaseError` — those live in `src/lib/supabase.ts` and `src/api/errors.ts` respectively. AFTER: Change the import to two separate imports: `import { supabase } from '../../lib/supabase'` and `import { transformSupabaseError } from '../errors'`. Do not change any function signatures or logic — only fix the import paths. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "database-api-C1-002",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "error_handling",
      "title": "auditTrail.ts imports transformSupabaseError from wrong module path",
      "file": "src/api/endpoints/auditTrail.ts",
      "description": "auditTrail.ts imports `supabase` from `../../lib/supabase` (correct) but imports `transformSupabaseError` from `../client` (wrong — client.ts does not re-export it). This will cause a build failure or runtime crash when any audit trail function is called.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/api/endpoints/auditTrail.ts, make these specific changes: BEFORE: Line 2 reads `import { transformSupabaseError } from '../client'`. The `../client` module (`src/api/client.ts`) does not export `transformSupabaseError`. AFTER: Change line 2 to `import { transformSupabaseError } from '../errors'`. The `transformSupabaseError` function is defined and exported from `src/api/errors.ts`. Do not change any other code in this file. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "database-api-C1-003",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "realtime",
      "title": "No React hooks wrapping Supabase queries — pages cannot use TanStack Query for caching, loading, or real-time invalidation",
      "file": "src/api/endpoints/projects.ts",
      "description": "The API layer defines raw async functions (getProject, getMetrics) but the codebase has no corresponding React hooks (useProject, useProjectMetrics) that wrap these in useQuery/useMutation with proper queryKeys. Without hooks, every page must manually manage loading/error/caching states, and the invalidation map in invalidation.ts is never triggered. The PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA requires skeleton loaders within 100ms and real-time updates within 2 seconds — both impossible without query hooks.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/api/endpoints/projects.ts, in the getProject and getMetrics functions: Add two exported React hooks at the bottom of the file. BEFORE: The file only exports raw async functions `getProject(projectId)` and `getMetrics(projectId)` with no React Query integration. Pages calling these must handle loading/error manually and have no cache invalidation. AFTER: Add `import { useQuery } from '@tanstack/react-query'` and `import { queryKeys } from '../queryKeys'` at the top. Then add: (1) `export function useProject(projectId: string) { return useQuery({ queryKey: queryKeys.projects.detail(projectId), queryFn: () => getProject(projectId), enabled: !!projectId, staleTime: 30_000 }) }` and (2) `export function useProjectMetrics(projectId: string) { return useQuery({ queryKey: queryKeys.metrics.project(projectId), queryFn: () => getMetrics(projectId), enabled: !!projectId, staleTime: 60_000 }) }`. Do not modify any existing function. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "database-api-C1-004",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "security",
      "title": "createScopedClient proxy does not filter soft-deleted rows (deleted_at IS NULL)",
      "file": "src/api/client.ts",
      "description": "The createScopedClient Proxy in client.ts auto-appends `.eq('project_id', projectId)` to every terminal operation, but never filters out soft-deleted rows. The backend schema specifies `deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ` on all major tables. Without `.is('deleted_at', null)` in the scoped client, queries return deleted records, violating data integrity and potentially leaking removed data to users.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/api/client.ts, in the createScopedClient function, inside the inner Proxy get handler for terminal operations: BEFORE: The handler calls `method.call(qbTarget, ...args).eq('project_id', projectId)` which scopes by project_id but does NOT filter out soft-deleted rows. AFTER: Change the return statement to chain an additional filter: `return method.call(qbTarget, ...args).eq('project_id', projectId).is('deleted_at', null)`. This ensures all queries through the scoped client automatically exclude soft-deleted records. Only apply this to SELECT operations — for INSERT, the deleted_at filter is unnecessary. Modify the condition to: `if (!skipScoping && typeof qbProp === 'string' && TERMINAL_OPS.has(qbProp)) { return (...args: unknown[]) => { const result = (qbTarget[qbProp as keyof typeof qbTarget] as TerminalMethod).call(qbTarget, ...args).eq('project_id', projectId); if (qbProp === 'select') { return (result as any).is('deleted_at', null); } return result; }; }`. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "database-api-C1-005",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "performance",
      "title": "getPortfolioMetrics in organizations.ts fetches full project rows instead of using count-only queries for RFI/punch aggregation",
      "file": "src/api/endpoints/organizations.ts",
      "description": "The getPortfolioMetrics function comment says it uses 'count-only queries' but the visible code structure shows it fetches from organizations, then does parallel queries. The metrics.ts version of getPortfolioMetrics fetches full rows with `.limit(200)` and joins projects, transferring potentially hundreds of full metric rows across the wire. For a GC with 50+ active projects this is a significant performance penalty. The function should use Supabase `.select('*', { count: 'exact', head: true })` pattern for count-only aggregation or rely on the RPC function referenced in the comment.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/api/endpoints/metrics.ts, in the getPortfolioMetrics function: BEFORE: The function queries `supabase.from('project_metrics').select('*, projects!inner(organization_id)').eq('projects.organization_id', orgId).limit(200)` which fetches up to 200 full metric rows with all columns. For portfolio-level counts this transfers excessive data. AFTER: Change the select to only include the columns needed for health scoring: `.select('project_id, open_rfis, overdue_rfis, open_punch_items, budget_variance_pct, schedule_variance_days, safety_incident_rate, projects!inner(organization_id)', { count: 'exact' })`. This reduces payload by ~80% while still allowing computeProjectHealthScore to run. Keep the `.eq('projects.organization_id', orgId)` and `.limit(200)` unchanged. Update the return type annotation if needed to match the narrower column set. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    }
  ],
  "invented_features": [],
  "competitive_intel": "Procore's API layer uses GraphQL with per-entity subscriptions and automatic optimistic cache updates. Their SDK handles retry, dedup, and offline queue natively. SiteSync's raw async functions without React Query hooks mean every consumer must manually manage loading/error/stale states — a significant regression from Procore's developer experience. Fieldwire's offline-first architecture with IndexedDB sync is also ahead. The scoped client Proxy pattern is clever but incomplete (missing soft-delete filtering and no mutation hooks for invalidation).",
  "summary": "The Database & API Layer has a solid architectural foundation — typed error hierarchy, project-scoped client proxy, query key factory, and centralized invalidation map. However, critical import path mismatches in metrics.ts and auditTrail.ts will cause build or runtime failures. The biggest structural gap is the complete absence of React Query hooks wrapping the raw async endpoint functions, meaning no page can leverage automatic loading states, caching, stale-time, or real-time invalidation — all of which are P0 requirements per the acceptance criteria. The scoped client also fails to filter soft-deleted rows, risking data leakage. Fixing the import paths, adding useQuery hooks to the most-used endpoints, and adding deleted_at filtering to the scoped client are the three highest-impact surgical fixes."
}
